pet.name: Saw
pet.special_needs: Needs more oxygen than other fishes..
pet.characteristics: High-oxygen fish typically include active species such as goldfish, plecos, or fast-swimming cyprinids that require robust aeration and water circulation. Care involves maintaining dissolved oxygen levels through air stones, powerheads, or surface agitation; regular water changes (25–50% weekly) to prevent oxygen depletion; cooler water temperatures when possible, as cold water holds more oxygen; and avoiding overcrowding. Monitor for gasping at the surface, lethargy, or rapid gill movement—signs of hypoxia. Diet should be species-appropriate (flakes, pellets, or vegetables depending on type) offered once or twice daily in amounts consumed within 2–3 minutes. Tank size should accommodate activity level and bioload; enrichment includes plants, hiding spots, and open swimming space. Handling is generally not recommended; observe fish behavior and water parameters (pH, ammonia, nitrite, nitrate) regularly.
pet.species_profile_summary: Lifespan varies widely by specific species (3–10 years typical for common high-oxygen fish in captive care); success depends on consistent aeration, water quality, and appropriate tank size.

age_context.summary: Lifespan varies widely by specific species (3–10 years typical for common high-oxygen fish in captive care), with longevity dependent on consistent aeration, water quality, and appropriate tank size.

Care involves maintaining dissolved oxygen levels through air stones, powerheads, or surface agitation; regular water changes (25–50% weekly) to prevent oxygen depletion; cooler water temperatures when possible, as cold water holds more oxygen; and avoiding overcrowding. Diet should be species-appropriate (flakes, pellets, or vegetables depending on type) offered once or twice daily in amounts consumed within 2–3 minutes. Tank size should be generous to support activity and gas exchange. Monitor for gasping at the surface, lethargy, or rapid gill movement, which indicate inadequate oxygen. goal: Create a complete pet care plan with daily, weekly, monthly tasks, reminders, and safe condition-aware guidance.

retrieved_passages.title: Medication Routine and Safety
retrieved_passages.content: Medication-related tasks in a home routine should focus on reminders, documentation, and observation, not dosage changes. A planning assistant can remind the owner to give medication exactly as already prescribed and to log whether the dose was completed.

retrieved_passages.title: Senior Pet Support
retrieved_passages.content: A senior pet plan should prioritize consistency over intensity. When time is limited, essential care such as feeding, hydration, medication reminders, and mobility support should be scheduled before optional enrichment.

retrieved_passages.title: Kidney Diet and Hydration Support
retrieved_passages.content: Owners should follow the veterinary feeding plan already prescribed and avoid changing food or supplement amounts without veterinary guidance. If a pet refuses multiple meals or shows sudden decline, the safe action is to contact a veterinarian.

retrieved_passages.title: Enrichment and Daily Monitoring
retrieved_passages.content: Observation tasks are useful for special-needs pets because they create a record of appetite, water intake, energy, and bathroom habits. These tasks should be short, specific, and easy to complete consistently.

retrieved_passages.title: Senior Pet Support
retrieved_passages.content: Older pets may benefit from routines that include medication reminders, slow feeding, and extra time for transitions. Owners should watch for changes in gait, confusion, appetite, and bathroom habits and report major changes to a veterinarian.

raw_recommendations.name: Feed Saw
raw_recommendations.notes: Offer species-appropriate food (flakes, pellets, or vegetables depending on type) in amounts consumed within 2–3 minutes. Once or twice daily.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Consistent feeding supports energy and health in senior fish. Portion control prevents water quality degradation, which is critical for high-oxygen species.

raw_recommendations.name: Monitor Saw for signs of oxygen stress
raw_recommendations.notes: Observe for gasping at the surface, lethargy, or rapid gill movement. These indicate inadequate oxygen levels.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Saw requires more oxygen than typical fish. Daily observation creates a record of behavior and helps detect oxygen-related distress early.

raw_recommendations.name: Check aeration and water circulation
raw_recommendations.notes: Verify air stones, powerheads, or surface agitation are functioning properly to maintain dissolved oxygen levels.
raw_recommendations.rationale: High-oxygen fish depend on robust aeration. Daily checks ensure equipment is working and oxygen levels remain adequate.

raw_recommendations.name: Perform 25–50% water change
raw_recommendations.notes: Change 25–50% of tank water weekly to prevent oxygen depletion and maintain water quality (pH, ammonia, nitrite, nitrate).
raw_recommendations.rationale: Regular water changes are essential for high-oxygen fish to prevent toxic buildup and maintain dissolved oxygen. Senior fish benefit from consistent water quality.

raw_recommendations.name: Monitor water parameters
raw_recommendations.notes: Check and record pH, ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate levels. Document results to track trends.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Water quality directly affects oxygen availability and Saw's health. Weekly monitoring creates a record for early detection of problems.

raw_recommendations.name: Observe Saw's appetite and energy
raw_recommendations.notes: Record whether Saw eats normally, shows lethargy, or displays unusual behavior. Note any changes.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Observation tasks for senior pets create a record of appetite and energy, helping detect decline early. Consistency is prioritized over intensity.

raw_recommendations.name: Verify tank temperature and oxygen conditions
raw_recommendations.notes: Maintain cooler water temperatures when possible, as cold water holds more oxygen. Avoid overcrowding to support gas exchange.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Temperature management and tank density directly impact dissolved oxygen levels for high-oxygen fish like Saw.

raw_recommendations.name: Minimal handling and observation
raw_recommendations.notes: Observe Saw's behavior and tank environment. Minimize handling to reduce stress.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Senior fish benefit from consistent, low-stress routines. Observation supports monitoring without unnecessary disturbance.
